# **Engineering Specification: Digital Surface Models Versus Digital Terrain Models in Fixed-AGL Flight Line Generation**

## **Introduction to Elevation Modeling in Aerial Surveying and Flight Management**

The precise execution of automated aerial surveys, photogrammetric missions, and synthetic aperture radar (SAR) collections relies entirely on the fidelity of the underlying topographical data ingested by the flight management system. Modern unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) and crewed surveying aircraft are routinely programmed to maintain a strict altitude Above Ground Level (AGL) to ensure consistent sensor geometry, uniform spatial resolution across the target, and safe obstacle clearance.1 The mathematical foundation of this terrain-following capability is the Digital Elevation Model (DEM), a generalized term encompassing two highly distinct and operationally critical subsets: the Digital Surface Model (DSM) and the Digital Terrain Model (DTM).2

A Digital Surface Model (DSM) represents the absolute highest reflective surface of the Earth. It captures the elevations of the bare ground alongside all persistent natural and artificial above-ground features, including forest canopies, buildings, bridges, and power lines.6 In essence, a DSM represents the "first return" surface—the top of whatever physical feature is present when viewed from a nadir perspective.2 Conversely, a Digital Terrain Model (DTM) represents the bare-earth geodetic surface, having been mathematically stripped of all persistent above-ground artifacts through sophisticated filtering and classification algorithms.2

The operational distinction between these two datasets becomes highly consequential when an aircraft transitions from open, bare terrain into environments characterized by dense forest canopies or high-rise urban infrastructure.2 For an aircraft programmed to execute a fixed-AGL flight line, the choice of elevation model dictates the reference surface upon which the altitude tracking algorithm operates. Utilizing a DSM forces the flight control system to treat the top of a forest canopy or a building roof as the ground, resulting in sudden vertical altitude commands that severely disrupt the aerodynamic stability of the aircraft and the optical geometry of the onboard sensors.10 Conversely, utilizing a DTM allows the aircraft to maintain a smooth, geodetically consistent flight path relative to the bare earth but introduces severe, potentially catastrophic collision risks if the commanded AGL is lower than the unmodeled canopy or building height.3

This engineering specification provides an exhaustive mathematical and aerodynamic analysis of the critical differences between DSMs and DTMs in the context of flight line generation. It explicitly models the transition from open terrain to dense forest, analyzing the severe impacts on aircraft pitch control algorithms, longitudinal dynamics, and camera footprint sizing. Furthermore, the analysis evaluates the specific radar penetration characteristics of the Copernicus DEM, a globally utilized X-band SAR DSM, across varying vegetation types. Finally, the report explores the computational architecture of FABDEM (Forest And Buildings removed Copernicus DEM), an advanced machine learning-derived synthetic DTM, to systematically assess the computational tradeoffs of utilizing a synthetic DTM versus a raw DSM for aviation safety and sensor performance.13

## **Flight Planning Architecture and Coordinate Geometry**

Before analyzing the aerodynamic and optical implications of terrain models, it is necessary to establish the computational architecture of modern flight line generation systems. Advanced flight management and aerial survey planning software, such as the open-source Rust-based IronTrack engine, utilize decoupled architectures to separate graphical presentation from intensive geospatial processing and photogrammetric math layers.17

In these systems, the ingestion of DEM data forms the foundation of terrain awareness. Global terrain datasets, such as the Shuttle Radar Topography Mission (SRTM), 3D Elevation Program (3DEP), or the Copernicus DEM, are typically ingested in the World Geodetic System 1984 (WGS84) geographic coordinate system.17 However, to perform geometrically accurate distance, area, and volume calculations required for photogrammetric swath planning, the compute engine dynamically projects the working areas into appropriate localized Coordinate Reference Systems (CRS), such as specific Universal Transverse Mercator (UTM) zones.17

To manage the massive data payloads associated with high-resolution global DEMs, flight engines employ dynamic terrain ingestion and caching mechanisms, organizing the elevation data into microtile structures.17 This allows the flight planning software to query elevations continuously along a projected 3D flight vector. The core output of this phase is the automatic calculation of optimal flight line altitudes based on the underlying elevation data, stored persistently in lightweight database-backed formats such as GeoPackage or GeoJSON.17 It is at this critical juncture—when the software interpolates the ![][image1]\-axis (elevation) coordinates to calculate the required flight altitude—that the selection of a DSM versus a DTM fundamentally alters the trajectory.

## **Mathematical Modeling of Camera Footprint and Sensor Geometry**

In aerial photogrammetry and remote sensing, the optical footprint and the Ground Sample Distance (GSD) are the primary metrics that dictate the spatial resolution, required flight line overlap, and overall quality of the spatial data collected.19 The geometry of the sensor is strictly dependent on the physical dimensions of the camera's complementary metal-oxide-semiconductor (CMOS) or charge-coupled device (CCD) sensor, the focal length of the optical lens, and the absolute vertical distance between the camera's optical center and the target surface.21

The theoretical ground footprint of a nadir-pointing camera is defined by the geometric principles of similar triangles, formed by the light rays passing through the principal point of the lens and projecting onto the image plane. Assuming a perfectly level flight attitude (zero roll, zero pitch), the ground footprint width (![][image2]) and the ground footprint height (![][image3]) are calculated using the following linear relationships 20:

![][image4]  
![][image5]  
Where:

* ![][image6] represents the physical width of the camera sensor (e.g., in millimeters).  
* ![][image7] represents the physical height of the camera sensor (e.g., in millimeters).  
* ![][image8] represents the focal length of the lens (e.g., in millimeters).  
* ![][image9] represents the flight altitude relative to the target surface (e.g., in meters).

The Ground Sample Distance (GSD), which defines the linear physical distance on the ground represented by the distance between two consecutive pixel centers in the digital image, is similarly dependent on the flight altitude.19 The GSD is calculated as:

![][image10]  
Where ![][image11] represents the image width in pixels.

### **The Effect of DSM vs. DTM on Optical Geometry During Forest Transitions**

The fundamental operational challenge in automated survey planning arises when the aircraft transitions from an open field to a dense forest canopy while actively tracking a fixed-AGL flight line. The reference altitude (![][image12]) utilized by the flight controller varies drastically depending on whether a DSM or a DTM is ingested into the flight planning software.23

If the flight management system utilizes a DTM, ![][image12] represents the distance to the bare earth. Assuming the aircraft is programmed to maintain a constant ![][image12] of 100 meters over a perfectly flat DTM, the flight path remains horizontal even as the aircraft passes over a dense, 30-meter tall coniferous forest. In this scenario, the distance to the bare earth remains 100 meters, ensuring that the theoretical GSD calculated for the bare earth remains constant.20 However, the physical distance between the camera and the top of the forest canopy is reduced to 70 meters (![][image13]). Consequently, the effective GSD at the canopy level becomes significantly finer, and the optical footprint shrinks proportionally.19 In photogrammetric surveys requiring high lateral and frontal image overlap to ensure dense point cloud generation through Structure from Motion (SfM) algorithms, this sudden shrinkage of the footprint at the canopy level may introduce severe data gaps, compromising the integrity of the 3D reconstruction.26

Conversely, if the flight management system utilizes a DSM (such as the Copernicus DEM), the reference surface actively includes the forest canopy. When transitioning from the open field to the 30-meter forest, the DSM dictates a sudden 30-meter vertical step in the terrain model.8 To maintain the fixed 100-meter AGL relative to the DSM, the aircraft must execute an aggressive climb, increasing its absolute geodetic altitude by 30 meters.

While this maneuver succeeds in maintaining a constant 100-meter distance to the top of the canopy, it severely degrades the sensor geometry relative to the bare earth.20 The absolute distance from the aircraft to the ground floor increases to 130 meters. Substituting this new altitude into the footprint equations demonstrates a proportional degradation in spatial resolution. If the original GSD at 100 meters was 2.0 cm/pixel, the new GSD at the ground level beneath the forest becomes:

![][image14]  
This 30% degradation in spatial resolution over the forest floor can invalidate the strict engineering specifications of a surveying contract, particularly in topographic mapping or civil engineering applications where the bare earth is the primary object of interest.4 Furthermore, the dynamic shift in the camera footprint size alters the overall swath width, requiring the flight line calculation logic to dynamically adjust the lateral spacing between adjacent flight lines to prevent coverage gaps.22

### **Rotational Distortions and the Shadow-Height Method**

The calculations above assume a perfectly level aircraft. However, the aggressive climb required to follow a DSM step introduces severe pitch angles (![][image15]). When the camera is subjected to high pitch or roll (![][image16]) angles, the image plane is no longer parallel to the ground. The optical footprint transforms from a rectangle into a trapezoid, introducing severe perspective distortions and varying GSD across a single image frame.21

This geometric distortion complicates advanced analytical techniques such as the shadow-height method, which is occasionally used in remote sensing to calculate the height of vertical structures based on the length of their shadows and the solar elevation angle.30 Rapid changes in sensor perspective and varying GSDs caused by DSM-induced altitude jumps severely degrade the pixel-level precision required for such optical derivations, emphasizing the necessity for stable, level flight.30

## **Aircraft Longitudinal Dynamics and Pitch Control Algorithms**

The transition over a forest canopy defined by a DSM does not merely affect optical geometry; it fundamentally disrupts the longitudinal flight dynamics of the aircraft.10 A flight management system attempting to follow a raw DSM must continuously alter the aircraft's pitch angle to track the erratic variations in the canopy surface.

### **State-Space Representation of Longitudinal Aircraft Dynamics**

To mathematically model the impact of a DSM-induced altitude step on the aircraft, the flight dynamics must be evaluated using a linearized, state-space representation of the longitudinal equations of motion.31 The aircraft is treated as a rigid body subject to aerodynamic forces (lift and drag) and moments (pitching moment). For small perturbations around a steady, level trim condition, the longitudinal dynamics are entirely decoupled from the lateral-directional (roll and yaw) dynamics.31

The longitudinal state of the aircraft is defined by the state vector ![][image17]:

![][image18]  
Where:

* ![][image19] represents the perturbation in forward velocity along the body x-axis.  
* ![][image20] represents the perturbation in the angle of attack (the angle between the relative wind and the wing chord line).  
* ![][image21] represents the pitch rate (the angular velocity about the lateral y-axis).  
* ![][image15] represents the perturbation in the pitch angle relative to the horizon.

The control input vector ![][image22] is defined primarily by the deflection of the elevator control surface:

![][image23]  
The continuous-time state-space equation governing the linear dynamic system takes the standard form:

![][image24]  
![][image25]  
The system matrix ![][image26] contains the longitudinal dimensional stability derivatives of the aircraft. These derivatives mathematically define how aerodynamic forces and moments change with respect to the state variables 35:

![][image27]  
Where:

* ![][image28] represent the speed derivatives affecting axial force, normal force, and pitching moment.  
* ![][image29] represent the angle of attack derivatives. ![][image30] is the critical static longitudinal stability derivative; a negative ![][image30] ensures the aircraft naturally pitches down when the angle of attack increases, providing inherent aerodynamic stability.35  
* ![][image31] represent the pitch rate derivatives. ![][image32] provides pitch damping, resisting the rotational motion of the aircraft.35  
* ![][image33] is gravitational acceleration, and ![][image34] and ![][image35] represent the steady-state trim velocity and pitch angle.

The input matrix ![][image36] contains the control derivatives representing the aerodynamic effectiveness of the elevator:

![][image37]  
The most critical element in matrix ![][image36] is ![][image38], the elevator control power derivative, which defines the magnitude of the pitching moment generated by a given elevator deflection.35

### **Terrain Following as a Step-Response Problem**

In automated terrain-following (TF) flight, the aircraft's autopilot receives an altitude reference command ![][image39] derived directly from the underlying DEM.39 The control system continuously calculates the altitude error ![][image40] and generates a pitch rate command ![][image41], which is executed via an inner-loop pitch-rate controller that actuates the elevator deflection ![][image42].10

When a DTM is utilized for flight planning, ![][image39] represents the smooth, continuous, and highly predictable gradients of the bare earth. The resulting temporal derivatives of the reference signal (the commanded climb rate ![][image43] and vertical acceleration ![][image44]) remain well within the aerodynamic and structural limits of the aircraft. The aircraft's natural dynamic modes—the phugoid mode (a long-period, lightly damped oscillation exchanging kinetic and potential energy) and the short-period mode (a highly damped, rapid oscillation in pitch and angle of attack)—remain stable and are easily suppressed by standard feedback control.33

However, when a raw DSM is utilized, the transition from an open field to a dense forest canopy introduces a severe mathematical discontinuity. The sudden appearance of a 30-meter wall of vegetation in the elevation model acts as a classic Heaviside step function ![][image45] in the altitude reference signal.44

To track this immediate 30-meter step input, the terrain-following autopilot calculates an instantaneous and massive altitude error. In classical control theory, whether utilizing a Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) controller, a Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR), or a pole-placement technique utilizing the Ackermann formula, this massive error signal demands an immediate and maximum allowable pitch rate (![][image21]).31

The inner-loop controller commands the elevator to deflect sharply (![][image46]). This generates a massive negative pitching moment (![][image47]), rapidly pitching the nose up and violently increasing the angle of attack (![][image20]).36 The rapid increase in ![][image20] results in a surge of aerodynamic lift (![][image48]), accelerating the aircraft vertically.33

### **Aerodynamic Consequences of DSM-Induced Step Inputs**

This aggressive maneuver subjects the airframe to extreme normal acceleration (g-forces).10 Simultaneously, the high angle of attack drastically increases induced drag, leading to a rapid decay in forward velocity (![][image19]).32 As the aircraft reaches the top of the canopy step, the altitude error approaches zero, forcing the controller to aggressively reverse the elevator deflection to arrest the climb and level out.42

This process results in severe altitude overshoot and oscillatory settling behavior, governed by the aircraft's damping ratio (![][image49]) and natural frequency (![][image50]).31 The time-domain response to a step input for a second-order underdamped system (![][image51]) exhibits a transient phase characterized by oscillation 36:

![][image52]  
Where ![][image53] is the damped natural frequency.36 During this violent transient phase, the aircraft's attitude is constantly shifting. Any optical sensors attempting to capture data during this period will experience severe motion blur and geometric distortion, rendering the imagery collected over the forest edge completely unusable.11 Furthermore, if the elevation step is too steep and the commanded pitch rate exceeds the aircraft's aerodynamic limits, the wing may exceed its critical angle of attack, resulting in an aerodynamic stall and a total departure from controlled flight.40

To mitigate these issues, advanced terrain-following systems increasingly rely on Model Predictive Control (MPC).10 Unlike PID or LQR controllers that react only to current or past errors, MPC applies receding-horizon optimization to anticipate future terrain gradients. By evaluating the trajectory over a future horizon ![][image54], the MPC algorithm can calculate a sequence of optimal control inputs subject to strict mathematical constraints on elevator magnitude (![][image55]) and actuator rate (![][image56]).10 While MPC smooths the trajectory by initiating the climb well before the forest edge is reached, it still results in a continuous shifting of altitude and degraded sensor footprint geometry over the canopy.10

## **The Copernicus DEM: X-Band Radar Penetration and Phase Center Quirks**

Given the aerodynamic hazards of following a surface model, flight planners must deeply understand the physical characteristics of the DSMs they utilize. The Copernicus DEM represents one of the most widely utilized global elevation datasets for automated flight planning, topographic mapping, and environmental modeling.18 It is a high-resolution Digital Surface Model (DSM) derived primarily from radar data acquired by the TanDEM-X mission between 2011 and 2015\.18

The TanDEM-X architecture utilized two twin synthetic aperture radar (SAR) satellites (TerraSAR-X and TanDEM-X) flying in close formation, typically separated by 120 to 500 meters.52 This configuration enabled single-pass bistatic cross-track interferometry, generating highly accurate, globally consistent topographical representations without the temporal decorrelation errors that plague repeat-pass satellite systems.52

However, the defining characteristic of the Copernicus DEM is its reliance on X-band radar. The X-band operates at a frequency of approximately 9.6 GHz, corresponding to a wavelength of roughly 3.1 centimeters.54 The physical interaction of these short-wavelength microwaves with complex volumetric targets—specifically dense forest canopies—introduces significant signal quirks that must be explicitly modeled to ensure aviation safety.57

### **The Scattering Phase Center (SPC) Bias**

The physical penetration depth of a radar signal into a vegetative medium is directly proportional to its wavelength.54 Long-wavelength radar systems, such as P-band (\~75 cm) or L-band (15–30 cm), easily pass through foliage and interact primarily with the heavy trunks and the bare ground beneath, producing a highly accurate terrain model.54 Intermediate C-band radar (4–8 cm) penetrates the upper leaves but scatters heavily off primary branches.54

In bare, rocky terrain or urban environments, the 3.1-centimeter X-band radar reflects directly off the uppermost solid surface, producing an elevation model that accurately represents the absolute physical height of the terrain or buildings with sub-meter vertical precision.8 However, when illuminating a forest, the 3-centimeter radar waves—which are roughly the size of a leaf—do not experience total reflection at the absolute top of the leaf canopy.54 Instead, the waves interact with millions of individual leaves, needles, twigs, and small branches, penetrating partially into the upper volumetric layers of the vegetation.9

The interferometric elevation derived from this complex interaction does not correspond to the physical top of the trees. Instead, it corresponds to the "Scattering Phase Center" (SPC) of the backscattered signal.15 Because the radar energy undergoes multiple volume scattering events within the top several meters of the canopy, the mean phase center is consistently located several meters below the true surface of the forest canopy.9

Consequently, the Copernicus DEM exhibits a systemic negative elevation bias over forested regions.63 It is a "leaky" DSM; it models a surface that is neither the bare ground (DTM) nor the true canopy top, but a phantom surface suspended within the upper branches of the trees.9

### **Quantitative Penetration by Vegetation Type**

The depth to which the X-band radar penetrates, and thus the magnitude of the negative elevation bias in the Copernicus DEM, is highly dependent on the vertical structure, density, and dielectric properties of the vegetation, as well as seasonal variations (e.g., leaf-on versus leaf-off conditions).9 Empirical analysis of TanDEM-X interferometric heights against high-resolution Airborne Laser Scanning (ALS) LiDAR reference data reveals distinct penetration characteristics across different forest types.

| Forest Type / Condition | Approximate X-Band Penetration (% of total tree height) | Absolute Mean Bias / Penetration Depth | Data Source Correlation |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Coniferous Forest (Evergreen)** | \~15% | \+1.9 m to 3.4 m | 9 |
| **Deciduous Forest (Summer / Leaf-On)** | \~18% | up to 6.2 m | 9 |
| **Deciduous Forest (Winter / Leaf-Off)** | \~24% | up to 8.2 m | 9 |
| **Sparse / Mixed Low-Relief Forest** | Variable (Density Dependent) | \~5.7 m to 6.1 m | 63 |

*Table 1: X-band SAR scattering phase center penetration depths derived from TanDEM-X acquisitions relative to true canopy top heights.*

This quantitative data highlights the severe unreliability and extreme danger of using a raw X-band DSM for low-level fixed-AGL flight operations.64 If a flight planner programs an aircraft to fly at a tight 5-meter clearance above the Copernicus DEM surface to maximize optical sensor resolution, the aircraft is virtually guaranteed to suffer a catastrophic collision. Over a winter deciduous forest where the phase center bias reaches 8.2 meters, a 5-meter commanded clearance above the DEM surface would physically place the aircraft 3.2 meters *inside* the upper branches of the tree canopy.9

Furthermore, because the penetration depth varies dramatically based on the localized species mix, tree cover percentage, and seasonal weather patterns, the elevation surface modeled by the Copernicus DEM over mixed forests is highly irregular.67 This introduces high-frequency spatial noise into the elevation profile, which acts as a continuous series of low-amplitude disturbance inputs to the aircraft's pitch controller, leading to sustained actuator wear, degraded fuel efficiency, and compromised optical stability.63

## **FABDEM: Machine Learning Architecture for Synthetic DTM Generation**

Recognizing the severe limitations of surface models for fixed-AGL flight routing, hydrological flood modeling, and geomorphological analysis, the geospatial engineering community required a globally consistent, high-resolution Digital Terrain Model. However, the removal of surface artifacts (trees and buildings) from a global radar DSM is an exceptionally complex computational challenge. Unlike dual-return LiDAR, which explicitly measures both the canopy top and the bare ground, the single-pass X-band radar data from TanDEM-X contains no definitive sub-canopy ground return information to process.65

To resolve this limitation, 'FABDEM' (Forest And Buildings removed Copernicus DEM) was developed. Released in 2022, FABDEM represents a vital alternative to the raw Copernicus DEM.14 It is a synthetic DTM generated at a 30-meter spatial resolution (1 arc-second) that systematically strips both vegetation and urban infrastructure biases from the original Copernicus GLO-30 dataset, providing a highly accurate estimation of the bare earth.71

### **The Random Forest Machine Learning Methodology**

The core computational engine behind the generation of FABDEM is an advanced machine learning approach utilizing a Random Forest regression model.74 Random Forest is a robust ensemble learning method that operates by constructing a multitude of decision trees during the training phase and outputting the mean prediction of the individual trees.77 This architecture relies on two critical mechanisms to prevent model overfitting and ensure robust generalization across diverse global landscapes:

1. **Bootstrap Aggregating (Bagging):** The algorithm creates random subsets of the training data with replacement, training individual decision trees independently on these unique subsets. This ensures each tree is exposed to slightly different data distributions.77  
2. **Random Subspaces:** At each node in the decision tree, only a random subset of predictor variables (features) is considered for making a split. This guarantees structural diversity among the ensemble of trees, reducing correlation and enhancing the predictive power of the model.77

To train the Random Forest model to accurately predict the vertical elevation bias (the exact height of the objects to be removed), the developers utilized high-precision, bare-earth airborne LiDAR DTMs sourced from 12 different countries.74 This training dataset represented a vast array of climatic zones, dense forest structures, and complex urban topologies, serving as the absolute "ground truth" target variable.74

### **Multi-Modal Predictors and Covariates**

Because the raw Copernicus DEM pixel lacks the contextual awareness required to differentiate a tall building from a natural hill, or a forest canopy from a rocky plateau, the Random Forest model ingests a comprehensive suite of multi-modal auxiliary datasets as predictor variables (features).16 These predictors provide the necessary ecological and structural context for the algorithm to accurately estimate the depth of the vegetation or infrastructure that must be mathematically excavated.16

| Covariate / Predictor Dataset | Physical Variable Provided | Spatial Resolution | Relevance to Bias Correction |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Copernicus GLO-30 DEM** | Uncorrected DSM Elevation | 30 m (1 arc-sec) | Baseline elevation surface to be corrected. |
| **Global Tree Cover (GLAD)** | Canopy Density (0–100%) | 30 m (1 arc-sec) | Identifies presence and density of forest areas to trigger vegetation removal logic. |
| **Global Forest Canopy Height (GEDI/ETH)** | Estimated Tree Height (m) | 10 m to 30 m | Provides absolute values for tree heights to define the magnitude of vertical excavation. |
| **World Settlement Footprint (WSF2019)** | Building Outlines / Urban Masks | 10 m (1/3 arc-sec) | Precise binary masking to isolate artificial structures for removal. |
| **GHS-BUILT-H / 3D-GloBFP** | Building Heights (m) | Variable | Informs the model of infrastructure volume and vertical displacement in urban zones. |
| **Terrain Slope (ETOPO / Derived)** | Topographic Gradient (degrees) | Variable | Helps the algorithm differentiate between steep natural terrain and vertical artificial structures. |

*Table 2: Key auxiliary datasets and predictor variables ingested by the Random Forest model to generate FABDEM.* 16

The algorithm processes these inputs through separate, dedicated correction models for forests and buildings. It performs a pixel-by-pixel regression to predict the vertical bias at each 30-meter grid cell.72 After the predicted bias is subtracted from the raw Copernicus DEM, extensive post-processing algorithms are applied. These include morphological image filtering and the deliberate filling of unrealistic pits or localized sinks created by algorithmic over-correction, ensuring a hydrologically continuous and geomorphologically plausible bare-earth surface suitable for flood routing and flight planning.72

### **Accuracy Improvements and the Evolution of DEM Machine Learning**

The application of this machine learning architecture yields dramatic improvements in vertical accuracy relative to the bare earth. Extensive statistical comparisons demonstrate that FABDEM roughly halves the mean absolute vertical error in forested regions compared to the original Copernicus DEM, reducing the error from approximately 5.15 meters down to 2.88 meters.14 In built-up urban areas, the mean absolute vertical error is reduced from 1.61 meters down to 1.12 meters.74

While FABDEM relies on pixel-by-pixel Random Forest regression, the field of DEM correction continues to evolve rapidly. Newer geometric deep learning models and hybrid vision transformer architectures, such as FathomDEM, utilize Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) within U-Net architectures to analyze errors using 2D spatial context as an inductive bias.75 These deep learning models evaluate the entire surrounding landscape rather than isolating individual pixels, leading to even more spatially coherent and robust corrections across complex terrain.75

## **Computational Tradeoffs and Aviation Safety**

The decision to utilize a raw DSM (Copernicus) versus a synthetic DTM (FABDEM) for automated flight line generation involves complex computational tradeoffs that directly impact aviation safety, mission efficiency, and data integrity.7

### **The Severe Risks of Raw DSM Routing**

As established, utilizing a raw DSM forces the aircraft's pitch control systems to aggressively track the undulating surfaces of forest canopies and buildings.10 The primary theoretical advantage of DSM-based routing is intrinsic obstacle avoidance.3 Because the elevation model includes the physical objects on the Earth's surface, a flight path calculated at a safe margin above a DSM inherently prevents collisions with buildings and trees.

However, this assumption is fatally flawed when utilizing radar-derived DSMs like Copernicus due to the X-band scattering phase center bias. Because the radar phase center rests several meters below the physical canopy top, an aircraft flying at a low AGL margin relative to the DSM will collide with the upper foliage.9 Furthermore, the computational overhead required by the autopilot to constantly calculate high-frequency pitch adjustments over irregular canopies results in excessive battery or fuel consumption, mechanical wear on flight control surfaces, and severely degraded optical sensor performance due to the constant shifting of the focal distance and GSD.22

### **The Synthetic DTM Advantage and Inherent Hazards**

Transitioning the flight planning architecture to utilize a synthetic DTM like FABDEM resolves the aerodynamic and optical issues entirely.24 The DTM provides a smooth, continuous mathematical representation of the bare earth. Flight lines generated over a DTM require minimal aggressive pitch maneuvers, allowing the aircraft to remain near its ideal aerodynamic trim state.10 The normal acceleration (g-loading) is minimized, energy efficiency is maximized, and the constant relative distance to the bare earth ensures that the camera's Ground Sample Distance (GSD) and swath width remain perfectly uniform throughout the survey.20

The critical computational tradeoff, however, is the complete forfeiture of intrinsic obstacle avoidance.7 A DTM is entirely blind to a 50-meter skyscraper or a 40-meter mature pine forest. If an aircraft is commanded to fly at a fixed AGL of 30 meters over a DTM, it will violently impact any surface feature exceeding 30 meters in height.3 Furthermore, because FABDEM is a machine learning derivative, it is subject to statistical predictive errors. In complex mountainous terrain with extremely dense vegetation, the random forest algorithm may still exhibit localized residual errors, meaning the "bare earth" estimate is not entirely infallible.71

### **Implementing the Canopy Height Model (CHM) for Dual-Layer Safety**

To mitigate the tradeoffs of both systems, advanced flight management specifications require the simultaneous ingestion of both models to construct a Canopy Height Model (CHM) or Normalized Digital Surface Model (nDSM).2 The mathematical derivation of this object-height array is highly computationally efficient:

![][image57]  
By computationally subtracting the FABDEM surface raster from the raw Copernicus DEM surface raster, the avionics system generates an isolated data layer representing only the precise height of the obstacles above local ground level.2

In a safe, optimized flight architecture, the primary flight line calculation and pitch control algorithms are anchored entirely to the DTM (FABDEM).24 This ensures smooth, predictable aerodynamic flight and perfectly constant optical geometry for ground mapping.24 Simultaneously, the CHM is processed in the background as an active obstacle-avoidance override layer.7

The flight computer utilizes a Model Predictive Control (MPC) algorithm to continuously scan the CHM array along the projected future flight vector.10 If the CHM array indicates that an upcoming obstacle height approaches the planned DTM-based flight altitude (plus a mandatory aerodynamic safety buffer), the autopilot dynamically triggers an evasive climb or route deviation well in advance of the obstacle.90 This sophisticated dual-layer approach harnesses the highly stable flight dynamics enabled by machine learning-derived bare-earth models while simultaneously mitigating the catastrophic collision risks inherent to blind DTM routing.

## **Conclusion**

The selection of an appropriate elevation model for automated flight line generation is a critical engineering decision that dictates the aerodynamic stability, sensor performance, and overall safety of aerial surveying platforms. Utilizing a raw Digital Surface Model like the Copernicus DEM forces the aircraft to aggressively track highly irregular canopy tops, resulting in violent pitch maneuvers, severe dynamic shifts in camera footprint sizing, and degraded spatial resolution at the ground level. Furthermore, the specific physical characteristics of the TanDEM-X X-band radar result in a scattering phase center that penetrates up to 24% into the vegetation canopy. This creates a "leaky" surface model that presents severe, unmodeled collision hazards if used for tight low-altitude clearance.

The advent of machine learning-derived synthetic Digital Terrain Models, notably FABDEM, provides a highly effective mathematical alternative. By employing Random Forest regression and extensive multi-modal predictors, FABDEM successfully excavates vegetation and urban artifacts, delivering a smooth, hydrologically sound bare-earth reference. While anchoring flight paths to a DTM drastically stabilizes aircraft pitch dynamics and ensures uniform optical geometry, it completely removes terrain obstacle awareness from the primary flight control loop.

Therefore, the optimal engineering solution mandates the integrated use of both the raw Copernicus DSM and the synthetic FABDEM DTM to generate a Normalized Digital Surface Model (nDSM) or Canopy Height Model (CHM). This dual-layer architecture permits the flight controller to utilize the DTM for stable, fixed-AGL navigation while relying on the derived height model to enforce absolute obstacle clearance margins, ensuring both optimal optical data collection and uncompromising aviation safety.

5. The FABDEM Outperforms the Global DEMs in Representing Bare Terrain Heights | Request PDF \- ResearchGate, accessed March 23, 2026, [https://www.researchgate.net/publication/374422393\_The\_FABDEM\_Outperforms\_the\_Global\_DEMs\_in\_Representing\_Bare\_Terrain\_Heights](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/374422393_The_FABDEM_Outperforms_the_Global_DEMs_in_Representing_Bare_Terrain_Heights)  
87. Everything you need to know about Digital Elevation Models (DEMs), Digital Surface Models (DSMs), and Digital Terrain Models (DTMs) \- UP42, accessed March 23, 2026, [https://up42.com/blog/everything-you-need-to-know-about-digital-elevation-models-dem-digital](https://up42.com/blog/everything-you-need-to-know-about-digital-elevation-models-dem-digital)  
88. DEM, DSM & DTM: Digital Elevation Model \- Why It's Important \- AEVEX Geodetics, accessed March 23, 2026, [https://geodetics.com/dem-dsm-dtm-digital-elevation-models/](https://geodetics.com/dem-dsm-dtm-digital-elevation-models/)  
89. Shopping for Digital Elevation Models \- Intermap Technologies, accessed March 23, 2026, [https://www.intermap.com/blog/shopping-for-digital-elevation-models](https://www.intermap.com/blog/shopping-for-digital-elevation-models)  
90. US5706011A \- Method for determining terrain following and terrain avoidance trajectories \- Google Patents, accessed March 23, 2026, [https://patents.google.com/patent/US5706011A/en](https://patents.google.com/patent/US5706011A/en)
